course_info.title: Healthcare Analytics
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Define the spectrum of healthcare analytics: descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive
Identify key analytics use cases across clinical and operational domains
Understand the data-to-insight pipeline in healthcare

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare organizations generate massive data volumes but extract little actionable insight. This course builds your ability to transform healthcare data into decisions that improve care and reduce costs.
key_concepts: Analytics maturity model: descriptive (what happened), diagnostic (why), predictive (what will happen), prescriptive (what should we do)
Clinical analytics: readmission prediction, sepsis early warning, population health
Operational analytics: ED throughput, OR utilization, staffing optimization
Financial analytics: revenue cycle, cost accounting, contract modeling
Analytics technology stack: data warehouse, BI tools, statistical software, ML platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through three real-world healthcare analytics dashboards showing clinical, operational, and financial use cases
setup_requirements: Python (Anaconda) with pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib installed

review.value: Using historical data and statistical models to forecast future outcomes such as patient deterioration or readmission risk
Most healthcare organizations are in descriptive/diagnostic stages; the opportunity is in advancing to predictive and prescriptive

learning_objectives: Identify major healthcare data sources and their characteristics
Apply data cleaning and preparation techniques to clinical datasets
Handle missing data, outliers, and data quality issues in health data
readings: Python for Data Analysis by Wes McKinney Ch. 5-7

lecture_notes.introduction: Analytics is only as good as the data it consumes. Healthcare data is notoriously messy. Learning to clean and prepare it is a foundational skill.
key_concepts: Data sources: EHR, claims, labs, imaging metadata, patient-generated, social determinants
Data quality assessment: completeness, accuracy, consistency, timeliness
Missing data strategies: listwise deletion, imputation (mean, median, MICE)
Outlier detection in clinical data (clinical vs. data-entry outliers)
Feature engineering from clinical data: comorbidity indices, time-based features
lecture_notes.demonstration: Python lab: load a messy hospital discharge dataset, profile it for quality issues, and clean it for analysis
setup_requirements: Sample discharge dataset with intentional quality issues

review.value: Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations: iteratively models each variable with missing values as a function of other variables to generate plausible replacements
Numerical score summarizing a patient's burden of chronic conditions (e.g., Charlson or Elixhauser) used as a risk adjustment feature

class_schedule.topic: Descriptive Analytics and Visualization for Healthcare
learning_objectives: Create effective data visualizations for clinical and operational audiences
Build healthcare KPI dashboards using appropriate chart types
Apply visualization best practices for communicating health data
readings: Storytelling with Data by Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic Ch. 2-4

lecture_notes.introduction: The most sophisticated analysis is worthless if stakeholders cannot understand it. Effective visualization translates data into actionable insights for clinicians and administrators.
key_concepts: Chart selection: when to use bar, line, scatter, box, heatmap, funnel charts
Healthcare dashboard design: KPI selection, layout, drill-down capability
Statistical process control (SPC) charts for quality monitoring
Color and annotation best practices for clinical audiences
Avoiding misleading visualizations in health data reporting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a hospital quality dashboard in Python (matplotlib/plotly) showing readmission rates, infection rates, and patient satisfaction trends
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with matplotlib, seaborn, plotly

review.value: Statistical Process Control chart showing a metric over time with mean and control limits, used to distinguish normal variation from special-cause variation
Effective dashboards: 5-9 KPIs maximum, clear visual hierarchy, comparison context (target, benchmark, trend), actionable drill-down

learning_objectives: Build logistic regression and decision tree models for clinical prediction
Evaluate model performance using appropriate clinical metrics
Understand the bias-variance tradeoff in healthcare prediction models
readings: Rajkomar A, et al. Machine Learning in Medicine. NEJM 2019

lecture_notes.introduction: Predictive models can identify patients at risk before adverse events occur. Building clinically valid, fair, and actionable models requires careful methodology.
key_concepts: Logistic regression for binary clinical outcomes (readmission yes/no)
Decision trees and random forests for interpretable risk stratification
Model evaluation: sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, AUC-ROC, calibration
Clinical significance vs. statistical significance in healthcare models
Bias-variance tradeoff and overfitting in small clinical datasets
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a 30-day readmission prediction model using logistic regression in Python, evaluate with AUC-ROC and calibration plot

review.value: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve: measures model's ability to discriminate between outcomes across all threshold values (0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect)
Whether predicted probabilities match observed frequencies - a model predicting 30% risk should see ~30% of those patients actually have the event

learning_objectives: Apply analytics methods to population health management
Implement risk stratification for patient panels
Analyze social determinants of health data alongside clinical data

lecture_notes.introduction: Population health analytics shifts the focus from individual patients to entire populations, enabling proactive care management and health equity analysis.
key_concepts: Risk stratification: identifying high-risk, rising-risk, and low-risk patients
Care gap analysis: finding patients overdue for preventive services
Chronic disease management analytics: A1c trends, BP control rates
Social determinants of health (SDOH) data and its analytical value
Geospatial analytics for community health assessment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a risk stratification model for a diabetic patient population using clinical and SDOH features
setup_requirements: Patient panel dataset with clinical and SDOH variables

review.value: Categorizing patients into risk tiers based on clinical data, utilization patterns, and SDOH to allocate care management resources effectively
Incorporating social factors (food access, housing, transportation) into analytical models to improve prediction accuracy and address health equity

class_schedule.topic: Ethics, Bias, and Governance in Healthcare AI
learning_objectives: Identify sources of bias in healthcare analytics and AI models
Apply fairness metrics to evaluate model equity across demographics
Understand governance frameworks for clinical AI deployment
readings: Obermeyer Z, et al. Dissecting Racial Bias in an Algorithm. Science 2019
WHO Guidance on Ethics and Governance of AI for Health

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare analytics and AI can perpetuate or even amplify existing health disparities if not carefully designed and monitored. Ethical AI is not optional in healthcare.
key_concepts: Sources of bias: historical data bias, selection bias, measurement bias, label bias
The Optum algorithm case: using healthcare cost as a proxy for health need encoded racial bias
Fairness metrics: equalized odds, demographic parity, calibration across groups
Model governance: validation, monitoring, update protocols, clinical oversight
FDA regulatory framework for AI/ML-based software as medical device (SaMD)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit a predictive model for demographic fairness: measure performance disparities across racial groups and apply bias mitigation

review.value: Systematic errors in AI models that produce unfair outcomes for certain patient groups, often reflecting historical inequities in healthcare delivery
Organizational processes for validating, deploying, monitoring, and updating clinical AI models to ensure ongoing safety and fairness

questions.question: A hospital discharge dataset has 15% missing values in the 'discharge disposition' field. Explain three strategies for handling this missing data and recommend which is most appropriate for a readmission prediction model, justifying your choice.
questions.answer: Three strategies: (1) Listwise deletion: remove rows with missing values. Simple but loses 15% of data and may introduce bias if missingness is not random (e.g., sicker patients more likely to have missing disposition). (2) Mode imputation: replace missing values with the most common disposition. Preserves sample size but underestimates variability and may distort relationships. (3) Multiple imputation (MICE): model disposition as a function of other variables and generate plausible values. For readmission prediction, MICE is recommended because disposition is likely related to other clinical variables (severity, procedures) and missing data may not be random. MICE preserves relationships between variables and properly accounts for imputation uncertainty.

questions.question: Explain when a Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart is more appropriate than a simple trend line for monitoring hospital-acquired infection rates. What do the control limits represent?
questions.answer: An SPC chart is more appropriate than a simple trend line because it distinguishes between common-cause variation (normal random fluctuation inherent in the process) and special-cause variation (unusual events indicating a real change). A trend line shows direction but cannot tell you if a monthly infection rate of 3.2% is meaningfully different from last month's 2.8%. SPC control limits (typically set at 3 standard deviations from the mean) define the expected range of common-cause variation. Points outside control limits, runs of 7+ points on one side, or trending patterns signal special-cause variation requiring investigation. This prevents overreacting to normal variation or missing genuine changes.

rubric.key: Correct distinction between common-cause and special-cause variation

questions.question: A hospital deploys a sepsis prediction model with 90% sensitivity and 60% specificity. In a population where 5% of patients develop sepsis, calculate the positive predictive value and explain its clinical significance for the care team.
questions.answer: Using Bayes' theorem: PPV = (Sensitivity x Prevalence) / [(Sensitivity x Prevalence) + ((1-Specificity) x (1-Prevalence))]. PPV = (0.90 x 0.05) / [(0.90 x 0.05) + (0.40 x 0.95)] = 0.045 / (0.045 + 0.38) = 0.045 / 0.425 = 10.6%. Clinical significance: only about 1 in 10 alerts will be true sepsis cases. The care team will experience 9 false alarms for every true positive. While the model catches 90% of sepsis cases (high sensitivity), the low PPV means alert fatigue is a serious concern. The team must design workflows that efficiently triage alerts without creating excessive burden.

rubric.key: Alert fatigue and workflow implications discussed

questions.question: Explain how incorporating social determinants of health (SDOH) data can improve a risk stratification model for a primary care patient panel. Give two specific examples of SDOH variables and their clinical relevance.
questions.answer: SDOH data improves risk stratification by capturing non-clinical factors that significantly influence health outcomes and healthcare utilization. Clinical data alone misses the ~80% of health outcomes driven by social and environmental factors. Example 1: Food insecurity (measured by screening questions or food desert mapping) - diabetic patients in food deserts have worse A1c control because they cannot access fresh produce, making them higher risk despite similar clinical profiles. Example 2: Transportation barriers (distance to clinic, public transit access) - patients without reliable transportation miss appointments and medication refills, increasing ED utilization and worsening chronic disease outcomes. Including these variables enables more accurate risk prediction and more targeted interventions.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of SDOH value in risk stratification
Two specific SDOH variables with clinical relevance

major_assignments.title: Clinical Predictive Analytics Project
major_assignments.description: Build, evaluate, and present a predictive analytics model for a clinical outcome using a real-world healthcare dataset. Include data preparation, feature engineering, model comparison, fairness analysis, and deployment recommendations.
objectives: Apply the full analytics pipeline to a healthcare prediction problem
Compare multiple modeling approaches and select the best performer
Evaluate model fairness across demographic groups
Develop deployment recommendations for clinical integration
deliverables: Jupyter notebook with complete analysis pipeline
Model comparison report with performance metrics

criteria: Multiple models compared (logistic regression, random forest, gradient boosting)
Proper evaluation metrics (AUC, calibration, clinical utility)
Bias mitigation attempted if disparities found
Rigorous analytics pipeline from data preparation through modeling
Thoughtful model comparison with appropriate metrics

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of analytics concepts, methods, and clinical application

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on Python analytics labs with healthcare datasets

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of analytics methods and data preparation

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including predictive modeling, population health, and AI ethics

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions mandatory. Lecture attendance expected. More than 2 unexcused lab absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Jupyter notebook submissions must include all outputs. No extensions without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts encouraged. All code and analysis must be individually produced. Identical code submissions treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All analysis must be original work. Use of AI coding assistants must be disclosed. Students must be able to explain every line of their code.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Python (Anaconda), Jupyter notebooks, scikit-learn, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn. GPU access provided for deep learning module.
